The next section I want to talk about is called the plan. This is where we break things down into three or four easy steps that people can take in order to do business with your clients. Let's go back to that sous vide cooker. How does Jule work, it says. Well, first you season and bag the food, then you drop it in the water, then you finish with a sear. That's it. That's how you create with a sous vide cooker. So anybody wondering whether they should buy this, it sounds so complicated. You know, what do you set the temperature at? How do you work this thing? When they break it down into three easy steps, it lifts the fog and it makes me want to buy it because I know that this will be easy for me. That's the purpose of this section of a website. And look at this beautiful way that Kyle at Schultz Photo School has laid out his plan. Three easy steps. One, you sign up for free. Two, you get a snack per day. And three, stick around if you want and get tips every other week. No spam, unsubscribe anytime. He really lays it out and lifts the fog about how you can get value from his website. Look at this module from CarMax. If you want to apply for financing, it's super easy. First find your car, two sear options, three make your decision. And then of course the call to action, try our finance calculator. All broken down into three easy steps. You know, buying a car can be really a scary thing. But when CarMax breaks it down into three easy steps, they are much more likely to get customer engagement. And not only that, if you scroll down the CarMax page currently, they actually talk about how you can sell your car through CarMax. They give you quick appraisals and offers. One, you bring in your car. Two, you let us appraise it. And three, you get your offer. That's it. Now of course we would know, of course we drive our car in and they're going to appraise it. But when we break it down into three easy steps, it lifts that fog, lifts the confusion and of course makes it much more likely that a customer will do business with us. Here's another client of ours, Beacon Wealth. It's their true wealth plan. First you request a meeting with a financial advisor and then you get a true wealth financial plan and then you invest according to your values. And they've seen great success. We want to break things down into three or four easy steps, all helping our customer engages. That's the plan module. Well next we have testimonies. And testimonies is one great way of demonstrating competence that we know what we're doing. So of course we're going to teach you in a later module how to gather testimonies, but this is where you would feature them as you wire frame a website. Of course it's very simple. I love what laser tag source has done here. Our customers love our service. They even gave their testimony section a heading and then they feature an endorsement from a happy client. You go to Cal Schultz Photo School and he's just got a few little endorsements. I want you to notice something though. Look how brief these endorsements are. Your clients are going to want long winded testimonies. Don't give it to them, right? You want them to be really short and scannable. Easy to read, easy to understand. Look at Equify LLC. It is a little bit of a longer paragraph, but instead of actually asking for a testimony from their client, they wrote the testimony for them. They actually said, here is how we helped Southwest Industrial Rigging solve a specific problem. It's another great way to do testimonies. We actually just write it, not from the voice of the client, but actually explaining here's what we did for a specific customer and here was their result. And of course here's Storybrand's website. We included the pictures, headshots of the people who are giving us their endorsement to make it more personal. Another great touch if you're including a testimonial module in a website. Next, and continuing with the theme of authority, we want to just include some company logos. Now you don't have to do this on every website, but it's a great way to demonstrate authority. When people see logos that they're familiar with, they trust the brand that they're interacting. Of course, I know about Intel. I know about Pantene. I've heard of Zaxby's. I've eaten there. Oh, these guys, trusted Storybrand. Of course we'll do business with them. What's great about including a logo section is it can just be a thin banner across the page. It doesn't take up much space, but as the customer is scanning the document with their squirrel brain, they get the idea, I can trust this brand. The next module is the explanation. Now here's where you actually get permission to be a little bit more long-winded. A lot of customers, after they're done scanning a page, they want to know more and they feel more comfortable making a purchase after they've read a lot of information. You can just give them a few paragraphs. What we do at Storybrand is we put a bold header, what makes Storybrand so different. Then we actually include a paragraph that really summarizes the Storybrand one page at the top. If people want to continue reading, they click Continue Reading and they can read the Storybrand one page in long form. Not everybody needs a paragraph. Not everybody needs a long explanation, but if your client really wants it, that would be the way to do it. You just have a Continue Reading button and then they can read it in long form, but don't waste the whole page with a bunch of text. It's going to feel too cumbersome as people scan down your page and they won't want to read it. They'll feel like it's too much work. First of course is the video. You can use a Storybrand one page to help clients create a video, a kind of script for a video. I would recommend your clients that they make a good sales video because most videos that most companies use are all about the company. It's not about the customer and it's really a lot of wasted space. Make sure it's a really good video. You can easily plug in a video as a module in an effective website. A couple of things I want you to notice. One is instead of just having a video, make sure that there's a header or some words or some text that makes me want to click play. Discover how the Storybrand process has helped businesses like yours quadruple in revenue. You will see a large uptick in people clicking the play button if you give them a reason to click the play button. Also explanatory videos are wonderful for clearing the fog for clients. How does laser tag source work? Yeah, I kind of wonder that. I mean, do I get stuff in the mail or when do I have to return it, all that kind of stuff? It's explained in the video, which makes it much more likely that I'm going to do business with you. Next is the cost or the failure of not doing business with you. We have to get the failure stakes into the website or there's no story, right? Here's how Equify LLC does it. They actually spell out all of their revenue streams and then they ask questions. Is your money hidden in your equipment? Is your money vulnerable to outside threats? Are you either underselling or overpaying for your equipment? Do you know the worth of your own assets? Is your money tied up in your real estate? Those are all questions basically saying, is something painful happening to you? And that's the essence of including the failure module in the wireframe of a website. You want people to really feel that toothache so they're much more likely to buy the medicine that you're selling. In fact, above the costs of our normal story brand workshop, the actual story brand foundation workshop, we spell out the failure stakes. How much is unclear messaging costing you? How many potential customers can't hear your offer in the sea of noise? How many of your events are half empty because people don't know why they should come? How many people are passing up your consulting? Can potential customers understand why they need your product or service? A lack of clarity may already be costing you a great deal. Listen, if you're working with a client who charges a lot of money for a product, you want to include a paragraph for this because what it does is it frames the cost. It seems like an expensive cost, but when I actually calculate what not doing this course is costing me, that seems like a much more reasonable price. The failure stakes are really important as a module in the wireframe of a website. Cal Schultz at Schultz Photo School includes this line, our kids grow up fast. Well, how's that the failure stake? If your kids are growing up and you're not getting great pictures, you're losing all of those memories. Now, failure is like salt in the recipe. You just want to pinch of it. Too much salt, it's going to make the website seem manipulative, but you do need to pinch of it or the story won't have any stakes. And of course you want price choices. And I say choices because a lot of businesses just have one price and sometimes that's the price of the product. And if you can bundle prices into packages, here's what Laser Tag Source does. They visually bundle their packages so that you want to buy almost like a value meal at a quick serve restaurant. You want to buy a box of this stuff, make it super easy, get it to my door. I don't have to think a lot in order to buy a package from Laser Tag Source. We actually have a 29.95 workshop and we wanted three different options, but it's a 29.95 workshop. And that buying increases when people have three options. So we just chose to give $100 off if two people come and $200 off if three people come. But visually it gave us the look of three price options on our website. And when people have a few options, buying increases. And then finally the junk drawer. Now the junk drawer is at the very bottom of the website, but it serves a great purpose. It gets rid of all the clutter on the website. Your clients are going to want to include all sorts of stuff at the top of the website. They're going to want to include frequently asked questions and about and contact and employment opportunities. If all that stuff is up in the header, it's confusing people as they scan through. Up in the header you really just want that direct call to action and maybe a transitional call to action. So get everything else that people will actually look for like contact and about us. Get that stuff in the junk drawer at the bottom of the page. The junk drawer gives you an opportunity as a copywriter to get rid of all that stuff that the client is demanding beyond the website and stick it down in the bottom so that you don't break the cardinal rule. And that is keep everything clear. Let's look at some before and after images of what websites look like after you help a customer create a Storybrand OnePage and wireframe a clear effective website. Remember Equify LLC. This was their website before I went in and helped them create a Storybrand OnePage and wireframed a new website. Look at how much clutter there is. And I don't know what Equify Asset Services is and I don't know what it means to build my business with Equify. There's so much small text. It's not scannable. And again, here's what we make it look like now. It's effective and it's producing better results. Here's another company called FITZEE out of Canada. FITZEE is a staffing company. They basically can act as your human resources department. They can put an ad up if you're looking for a new hire. They can then interview everybody who responds to that ad and deliver to you four or five really great candidates after they even test them to make sure that they're great candidates. On the left, you see their old website. Now that is extremely complicated. But on the right, you see what their website turned into after they created a Storybrand OnePage and filtered their message. Not only that, but they went from about us pages that was bragging about them making themselves the hero to pages that were more about the customer, asking the customer about the pain points they experienced that FITZEE knew they could resolve. And what was the result? It was staggering. So they created their Storybrand OnePage in December and began to turn that into better marketing collateral. From December to July, they saw an 118% growth in their overall company, in their overall revenue. They doubled their revenue in only eight months. That is amazing. They saw a 276% increase in paying customers. Why? Because customers finally knew what they did and they knew how to engage. They understood the pain that FITZEE could help them resolve. And so they engaged in order to get medicine to resolve that pain. It was all very clear in their marketing collateral. They saw a 161% increase in active customers posting to their job board. These are phenomenal, phenomenal returns on the minor investment of just getting clear. Now again, they didn't throw a bunch of money at this problem in terms of hiring ad agencies and expensive complicated strategies. They just thought about it and got clear. And that's the work that you want to do as a Storybrand copywriter. You want to really clarify that message, get rid of most of the text, and make it incredibly understandable and easy to access on a website. As you work with the client, spend some time just wireframing that website. You'll put a header here and maybe you'll go with a value proposition and maybe you'll go with a cost. And then later you'll say, you know, I think I just want to put the cost up here and the value proposition. Sit with it for a while after you create it. Maybe you've done it on a big whiteboard. You can either take a picture of it and send that to your client or draw it out on a piece of paper or lay it out on a piece of software, whatever it is. But wireframing that website and getting all of the text right is going to be the first huge step in seeing a return for your client or for yourself.
